coredata.dc:description: Cellular redox homeostasis is highly dependent on endogenous antioxidant systems, including superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT), and glutathione peroxidase (GPx). The nanozymes mimicking the catalytic activities of natural antioxidant systems serve as potential candidates for the therapy of reactive oxygen species (ROS)-related diseases. However, previously reported metal-containing nanozymes possessed unsatisfactory catalytic activity and inevitable peroxidase activity, limiting their translational applications. Therefore, inspired by the superior ROS scavenging activities of natural polyphenols and metal-free carbon dots (CDs) with ultra-small size, phenol-functionalized CDs that mimic endogenous antioxidant system have been fabricated. The SOD activity was determined at an extremely high level (18187 U/mg), which was substantially comparable to natural SOD. Notably, this study reveals for the first time that CDs, as selenium-free nanomaterials, show robust GPx activity in vitro. The theory calculation suggests that the bond dissociation of phenols on CDs is considerably lower than selenocysteine of natural GPx, which facilitates the formation of peroxide intermediate to initiate GPx-like reactions. Furthermore, the kidney-targeting and intracellular antioxidant mimicking properties contribute to the effective alleviating cisplatin-induced acute kidney injury via suppressing oxidative stress-mediated ferroptosis in vivo. These results provide an effective polyphenol-functionalized strategy for synthesizing metal-free multifunctional antioxidant nanozyme to treat ROS-related diseases.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of CDs and CDs@PEG. (A) ROS scavenging capacities of CDs with different ratios of GA/PEI1800. Mean ± S.D., n = 3. (B) Scheme for preparation of CDs@PEG. (C, D) HRTEM images of CDs (C) and CDs@PEG (D). Scale Bar: 20 nm. Inset: particle size distributions. (E) XRD patterns of CDs and CDs@PEG. (F) FTIR spectra of CDs and CDs@PEG. (G, H) High-resolution N1s (G) and O1s (H) XPS spectra of CDs and CDs@PEG. Curve fitting was used for peak identification.

ROS scavenging capacity of CDs and CDs@PEG. (A) SOD-mimic activity of CDs. (B) Fitted SOD enzymatic activity of CDs@PEG. (C) O2
− scavenging capacity of CDs@PEG analyzed by EPR spectroscopy. (D) GPx-mimic activity of GA, CDs, and CDs@PEG (100 μg·mL−1) at serial time points determined colorimetrically using a GSH-Px-DTNB assay. (E) GPx-mimic activity of gradual doses of CDs@PEG and GA (100 μg·mL−1) determined colorimetrically using a GSH-Px-DTNB assay. (F) High-resolution extracted ion chromatograms of GSH, GSSG, and catalytic products. (G) Bond dissociation enthalpies of phenolic OH of CDs and Se-H of Selenocysteine (Se-Cys) analyzed by DFT calculations. (H) Free energy diagram for the reaction of CDs or Se-Cys with ROOH to form peroxide intermediate. (I) The CAT-mimic activity of CDs@PEG with different concentrations determined by the O2 generation. (J, K) ·OH radical scavenging ability of CDs@PEG analyzed using colorimetric assay (J) and EPR spectroscopy (K).

CDs@PEG mitigates ROS-induced cellular injury in vitro. (A-C) Effects of CDs@PEG on viability of HK-2 cells incubated with H2O2 (400 μM) (A), LPS (20 μg/mL) (B), and CP (20 μM). Mean ± S.D., n = 3. (D) FL images of HK-2 cells double-stained by Calcein-AM/PI. Scale bar: 100 μm. (E) FL images of DCFH-DA-stained HK-2 cells. Scale bar: 100 μm. (F) FL images of JC-1-stained HK-2 cells. Scale bar: 100 μm. (G) Flow cytometric cell apoptosis analysis in HK-2 cells.

CDs@PEG inhibits lipid peroxidation and reduces cytosolic labile iron in HK-2 cells in vitro. (A) FL images of HK-2 cells stained with Liperfluo probes. (B) Relative FL intensity in (A). (C) Relative LPO levels in HK-2 cells determined using a commercial LPO kit. (D) FL images of HK-2 cells stained with MBB. (E) Relative FL intensity in (D). (F) Relative NADPH levels in HK-2 cells determined using a NADP/NADPH kit. (G) FL images of HK-2 cells stained with Ferro-Orange. (H) Relative FL intensity in (G). (I) Flow cytometric analysis of labile iron levels in HK-2 cells. Mean ± S.D., n = 3. #P < 0.05, ##P < 0.01, and ###P < 0.001 versus the control group and *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, and ***P < 0.001 versus the CP group.

In vivo biocompatibility and biodistribution of CDs@PEG in healthy and cisplatin (CP)-injected AKI (CP-AKI) mice. (A) The major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidneys) were stained by H&E 24 h after intravenous administration of CDs@PEG. (B, C) Representative IVIS images of organs harvested from CDs@PEG-injected healthy (B) and CP-AKI (C) cohorts. (D, E) Average fluorescence intensities of organs harvested from healthy (B) and CP-AKI (C) mice upon CDs@PEG injection at selected time points (0–24 h). Mean ± S.D., n = 3.

CDs@PEG alleviates cisplatin (CP)-induced AKI in vivo. (A) In vivo study design. (B) H&E staining of representative kidney sections. Triangles indicate the formation of casts (Scale bar: 100 or 50 μm). (C, D) Serum BUN and CRE levels (### P < 0.001 versus the Control group; ***P < 0.001 versus the CP group; n = 6). (E) Confocal images of representative kidney sections, stained with DHE (red) and DAPI (blue) (Scale bar: 50 μm). (F) Confocal images of representative kidney sections, stained with TUNEL (green) and DAPI (blue) (Scale bar: 50 μm).

CDs@PEG suppresses ferroptosis in the kidney of AKI mice. (A) TEM images of representative renal tissues (Scale bar: 1 μm or 500 nm). (B) Immunohistochemical staining of 4-HNE in kidney slices (Scale bar: 50 μm). (C) Renal GPx activities (C), GSH levels (D), and MDA contents (E) in mice. (F-H) Protein expression levels of ACSL4/PTGS2 (F), GPX4/xCT (G), and FTH1/TfR1 (H) in kidney samples of CP-AKI mice assessed by western blotting assay and densitometric analysis. Mean ± S.D., n = 3; # P < 0.05, ## P < 0.01, and ### P < 0.001 versus the control group and *P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, and *** P < 0.001 versus the CP group.

Scheme for the fabrication of Phenol-functionalized CDs mimicking the intracellular antioxidant system. And the Phenol-functionalized CDs show promising efficacy in a cisplatin-induced AKI mouse model via multiple-enzyme-mimicking antioxidant properties and ferroptosis suppression.

head.ce:title: Metal-free multifunctional nanozymes mimicking endogenous antioxidant system for acute kidney injury alleviation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cellular redox homeostasis is highly dependent on endogenous antioxidant systems, including superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT), and glutathione peroxidase (GPx). The nanozymes mimicking the catalytic activities of natural antioxidant systems serve as potential candidates for the therapy of reactive oxygen species (ROS)-related diseases. However, previously reported metal-containing nanozymes possessed unsatisfactory catalytic activity and inevitable peroxidase activity, limiting their translational applications. Therefore, inspired by the superior ROS scavenging activities of natural polyphenols and metal-free carbon dots (CDs) with ultra-small size, phenol-functionalized CDs that mimic endogenous antioxidant system have been fabricated. The SOD activity was determined at an extremely high level (18187 U/mg), which was substantially comparable to natural SOD. Notably, this study reveals for the first time that CDs, as selenium-free nanomaterials, show robust GPx activity in vitro. The theory calculation suggests that the bond dissociation of phenols on CDs is considerably lower than selenocysteine of natural GPx, which facilitates the formation of peroxide intermediate to initiate GPx-like reactions. Furthermore, the kidney-targeting and intracellular antioxidant mimicking properties contribute to the effective alleviating cisplatin-induced acute kidney injury via suppressing oxidative stress-mediated ferroptosis in vivo. These results provide an effective polyphenol-functionalized strategy for synthesizing metal-free multifunctional antioxidant nanozyme to treat ROS-related diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Reactive oxygen species (ROS), the inevitable products of aerobic metabolism, serve multifarious physiological regulatory roles in the biological system, such as signal transduction and programmed cell death [1]. However, the imbalance between ROS elimination by the natural antioxidant system and ROS generation would contribute to cellular oxidative stress and its advanced pathological changes, such as inflammation, carcinomas, etc [2]. In order to maintain biological redox homeostasis and prevent the body from oxidative damage, the endogenous antioxidant defense system consists of serial natural enzymes and antioxidative small molecules that synergistically scavenge excessive free radicals [3]. The main cellular antioxidant machinery comprising of superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT), glutathione peroxidase (GPx), and small molecules, such as glutathione (GSH) [4]. SOD serves as the first line of the natural antioxidant system that catalyzes the dismutation of O2
−, as the initial source for producing other toxic ROS into H2O2 and molecular oxygen [5]. And H2O2 is also toxic to the biological system, which can be converted to H2O and O2 by CAT or to H2O by GPx with GSH as cofactors [6]. Moreover, elevated production of ROS robustly drives the accumulation of lipid peroxides (ROOH) in the presence of “unchaperoned” iron that initiates ferroptosis [7]. However, the detoxication of ROOH can only be realized by the natural GPx4, which serves as the suppressor of ferroptosis [8].

Although members of the natural antioxidant system are effective biocatalysts, they are sensitive to harsh physiological conditions, unstable to modify, and expensive, which retarded their translational applications [9]. In order to address these issues, researchers designed numerous enzyme-mimicking systems by nanozyme, defined as nanomaterials with intrinsic enzyme-like activities [10–12]. Herein, ROS scavenging nanozymes mimicking endogenous antioxidant systems serve as potential candidates for the therapy of ROS-related diseases [13]. Qu et al. previously fabricated a novel multifunctional nanozyme cooperative system consisting of V2O5 and MnO2 with dopamine as a linker to mimic the intracellular antioxidant enzyme-based defense systems [14]. Also, the intracellular antioxidant enzyme was mimicked by the MnO2-encapsulated selenium-melanin or two-dimensional carbide MXene nanozyme [15,16]. Nevertheless, the unsatisfied catalytic activities and inevitable peroxidase activity of these metal-containing nanozymes [17,18] further limited their applications. Therefore, the development of metal-free nanozymes that can mimic the intracellular antioxidant system is urgently required.

However, metal-free nanozymes mimic the intracellular antioxidant nanozyme has rarely been reported. Recently, the ROS scavenging features of ultra-small carbon nanodots (CDs) and their applications in metabolic, aging, and inflammatory diseases have recently been illuminated [19–23]. As natural antioxidants, polyphenols possess noticeable biological effects, including multifarious pharmacological activities and high physiological stability [24]. Polyphenols-based nanomaterials have aroused great attention and have been synthesized via different strategies [25]. Therefore, inspired by the superior ROS scavenging activities of natural polyphenols and CDs with adjustable surface modification, phenol-functionalized and metal-free CDs with efficient SOD, GPx, CAT like activity that can mimic the endogenous antioxidant system have been fabricated via the typical Michael addition reaction between natural polyphenol as the surface ligand and polyethyleneimine as the carbonized source (Scheme 1 ).

Notably, the SOD activity of CDs was determined to be extremely high (18187 U/mg), which was higher than previously reported carbon nanomaterials. Furthermore, the GPx-catalytic activity of CDs@PEG has firstly been reported in conventional selenium-free carbon nanomaterials. Owing to the highly effective intracellular antioxidant system mimicking features and ultrasmall size targeted to the kidney, CDs@PEG displayed renal selectivity and significant therapeutic efficacy in a cisplatin-induced acute kidney injury (CP-AKI) mouse model by suppressing ferroptosis via multiple-enzyme-mimicking and superior natural antioxidant system (Scheme 1). This study is expected to facilitate the rational design of a high-efficient, low-cost, and metal-free CDs-based antioxidative system for treating AKI and other ROS-related diseases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of polyphenol functionalized CDs and CDs@PEG
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve effective therapy of AKI, the typical natural plant polyphenols were selected as raw materials to synthesize ultrasmall phenolic group functionalized CDs with renal selectivity and ROS scavenging capacity. The oxidation of catechol and galloyl groups contributes to the formation of quinones groups, which can react with amino or aldehyde groups via Schiff base and/or Michael addition reactions. Furthermore, the antioxidant activity of natural polyphenols is highly related to the substituent groups. The lower OH bond dissociation energy of phenolic groups favors the H-atom donation, which results in better antioxidant ability [26]. Since the superior electron donating ability of amino, PEI with abundant amino was selected as the carbon source to facilitate the formation of phenolic-functionalized CDs.

Emerging evidence demonstrates that gallic acid (GA, 3,4,5-trihydroxybenzoic acid) is a naturally occurring small-molecular polyphenol compound with antioxidant properties and applicable for the preparation of phenolic-based engineering ultra-small nanoparticles with better pharmacokinetics [27]. Here, GA was chosen as a model molecule for validating the feasibility of polyphenol-functionalized CDs fabrication aforementioned. The influence of the mass ratio of PEI/GA was firstly investigated. Although the simple mixing of GA and PEI tended to aggregate in deionized water, it gradually turned to clarification, and the corresponding color turned yellow and brown with the addition of NaOH. Moreover, the alkaline conditions favor the Michael addition reactions that contribute to the formation of CDs. When the amount of PEI1800 was less than GA, apparent precipitates were observed after the hydrothermal treatment, implying excessive crosslinking at these ratios. Once the mass ratio of PEI1800/GA was larger than 1, a transparent brown solution with obvious fluorescence (FL) was obtained, indicating the successful formation of CDs. The mass ratio of polyphenols/PEI was further optimized based on their ROS scavenging activity. Since the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) induced by horseradish peroxidase (HRP) is highly correlated with the ROS, the distinct colorimetric reaction was utilized to reflect their ROS scavenging ability [28]. When the ratio of GA/PEI1800 increased from 1:1 to 3:4, the aggregation was gradually improved, and their ROS scavenging abilities were also enhanced. With the further increase of PEI1800, their ROS scavenging ability decreased (Fig. 1 A and S1). The surface zeta potential of CDs obtained from different ratios of GA/PEI was positive and relatively close to each other, probably due to the excess crosslinking at higher ratio of PEI ( Fig. S2), indicating that the electrostatic adsorption of positive TMB on CDs was not the decisive factor resulting in the different ROS scavenging capacity. Compared to the other groups, CDs-GA3/4 had higher relative oxygen content (Fig. S3), which may be attributed to the substantially higher amounts of phenolic groups than the majority of other samples. In accordance with their ROS scavenging capacity, the redox potential of CDs-GA3/4 determined by cyclic voltammetry is smaller than the other groups (Fig. S4 and Table S1).

Subsequently, CDs conjugated with other polyphenols, including tannic acid (TA), caffeic acid (CA), resveratrol (RES), and epigallocatechin (EGCG), were prepared to estimate the influence of different polyphenols on the antioxidant capabilities of CDs (Fig. S5A). The ROS scavenging ability of TA-derived CDs (TA-CDs) decreased obviously, which might be attributed to the much larger conjugated system of TA that contributed to the obvious aggregation of the TA-CDs with vast inert phenolic groups (Fig. S5B). The limited ROS scavenging capacity of CA-CDs and RES-CDs is related to the lack of a typical 3,4,5-trihydroxyben structure present in GA. The EGCG-CDs derived from EGCG with abundant 3,4,5-trihydroxyben structure exhibited superior ROS scavenging activity. However, the large MW of EGCG would contribute to slightly lower ROS scavenging activity than GA (Fig. S5B). Therefore, CDs prepared by the ratio of GA/PEI1800 = 3/4 were applied in the following experiments. The influence of molecular weight (MW) of PEI on the ROS scavenging capacity of CDs was also investigated. None CDs were successfully obtained using PEI20K and GA due to overlarge MW that would contribute to the formation of precipitates rather than small nanoparticles. However, compared with GA-CDs prepared by PEI1800, ROS scavenging activity was retarded by that derived from PEI600, possibly due to the over-crosslinking between PEI600 and GA (Fig. S5C). In order to extend the blood circulation time and decrease the non-specific adsorption, the PEG2000 was covalently anchored onto the surface of CDs for CDs@PEG preparation (Fig. 1B).

CDs and CDs@PEG were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Large amounts of mono-disperse spherical particles were homogeneously distributed in TEM images (Fig. 1C, D). The average sizes were 4.34 ± 0.56 and 4.86 ± 0.86 nm for CDs and CDs@PEG, respectively. CDs and CDs@PEG showed obvious crystallinity with a lattice spacing of about 0.21 nm, related to the (100) facets of the graphite structure. Moreover, an apparent diffraction peak at about 25.4° was observed in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CDs, corresponding to facers parallel and perpendicular to the sp2 carbon layer of graphite (Fig. 1E). However, with the conjugation of PEG, the diffraction peaks related to the (002) facets shifted obviously to 22.6°, which could be attributed to an enlarged interlayer distance resulting from the steric hindrance of PEG chains.

The surface chemistry was characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The intense absorption band of CDs at 3424 cm−1 implies the presence of abundant phenolic hydroxyl groups and amino groups (Fig. 1F). Meanwhile, the two absorption peaks at 2966 and 2854 cm−1 were related to the stretching vibration of C-H, derived from the alkane chain of PEI. Several peaks between 1627 and 1457 cm−1 demonstrate the presence of moderate content of amide bonds on the surface of CDs. As long as conjugation with the PEG, the absorption band at 2962 and 2844 cm−1 became more intense due to an abundant alkane chain of PEG. Furthermore, the peak at 1523 cm−1 disappeared, and that at 1618 cm−1 shifted to 1614 cm−1, derived from the stretching vibration of C = O, indicating the successful formation of amide bonds. Consistently, the appearance of two peaks at 1076 and 1020 cm−1 of typical stretching vibration of C-O-C confirmed the presence of PEG. As for the XPS spectra, several peaks at 284.8, 399.0, and 532.0 eV were observed, indicating the presence of C, N, and O elements (Fig. S6A). Compared with CDs, the relative oxygen content increased obviously, and that of nitrogen decreased apparently due to the conjugation of NHS-PEG via an amide bond (Fig. S6B). The high-resolution spectra of N1s and O1s were further analyzed to verify the changes in the surface group (Fig. 1G). The N1s spectra of CDs and CDs@PEG could be deconvoluted into C = N-C, amide N, and amino N. Their related O1s spectra could be fitted into O = C and HO-C, respectively (Fig. 1H). The relative content of each surface component is listed in Table S2. The decrease of amino confirmed the formation of amide, and the increase of the C-O demonstrated the presence of abundant alkoxy from PEG. The alternation of the surface zeta potential of CDs from positive to −4 mV (Fig. S6C) also demonstrated the PEG conjugation, which assures a longer blood circulation time.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs showed two peaks at 211 and 265 nm, which could be attributed to the n → π* transition of the CO bond and π → π* transition of the aromatic sp2 bond. With the conjugation of PEG, the absorption peak shifted to 304 nm, and the absorption band between 300 and 700 became more intense (Fig. S7A). Moreover, CDs exhibit a pronounced FL peak at 540 nm, demonstrating the formation of CDs (Fig. S7B). The FL of CDs@PEG could be fitted by a second exponential decal model, and the average lifetime was determined to be 3.91 ns (τ1 = 4.77 ns, τ2 = 8.81 ns) which is close to that of CDs (Fig. S7C). In addition, the FL features of CDs were quite stable in complex physiological media, including PBS, Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM), and fetal bovine serum (FBS) (10 %) within two weeks (Fig. S7D). The FL intensity of CDs could remain constant after continuous exposure to LED light for 2 h (Fig. S7E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro ROS scavenging activity of CDs@PEG
ce:section.ce:para: The ROS scavenging performance of CDs@PEG was evaluated in several aspects, especially their enzyme-mimicking features, such as the SOD, GPx, and CAT, which are essential for ROS elimination [29]. The total antioxidant capacity assay of CDs@PEG was performed based on the colorimetric reaction of 2, 2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS). The oxidized product ABTS+ with the green color could be erased by various existing antioxidants, which reflects the total antioxidant capacity via the typical adsorption at 734 nm. As shown in Fig. S8A, CDs@PEG inhibited the absorption at 734 nm in a concentration-dependent manner. About 91.2 % inhibitory ratio of ABTS+ could be realized by only 25 μg/mL of CDs@PEG, which is superior to previously reported CDs-based ROS scavenging agents [30]. Moreover, the DPPH assay confirmed the excellent antioxidant activity of CDs@PEG (Fig. S8B, C), which is also crucial for their H atom donating ability.

The total SOD mimicking properties of CDs@PEG were evaluated using the WST-8 colorimetric methods. WST-8 can be reacted with O2
− derived from the oxidation of xanthine by xanthine oxidase (XO) to generate formazan dye with typical absorption at 450 nm. The generation of formazan dye can be inhibited by SOD. The inhibitory ratio of O2
− by CDs@PEG was also concentration-dependent, and about 74.8 % O2
− could be eliminated by CDs@PEG (25 μg/mL). Moreover, compared with the natural SOD (66.7 % inhibitory ratio can be realized by 60 U/mL natural SOD), CDs@PEG displayed a more enhanced ability to eliminate O2
− (Fig. 2 A). Interestingly, CDs@PEG showed a similar inhibitory ratio to GA (Fig. S8D), demonstrating the superior SOD-like activity of CDs@PEG. Furthermore, the SOD-like activity of CDs@PEG was pH-dependent, where it gradually decreased within pH from 5 to 10 (Fig. S8E). The SOD mimicking activity of CDs@PEG was determined to be 18,187 U/mg (Fig. 2B), which was comparable to natural SOD and greater than the SOD-like activity of previously reported carbon nanomaterials-based SOD mimetics (Table S3). The extremely high SOD-like activity of CDs may be derived from abundant surface phenolic-like groups. The relative lower bond dissociation energy of phenolic-like groups facilitates H atom donation. As the active sites, the phenolic hydroxyl groups serve as H-atom donors to capture O2
− through hydrogen bonds and their H-atom donating capacity [26,31]. The SOD-like activity of CDs@PEG was further investigated by the electron spin resonance (ESR), a sensitive and accurate method for O2
− detection, using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline A'-oxide (DMPO) as the spin trapping reagent to produce the DMPO/OOH adduct. With the increased concentration of CDs@PEG, the ESR signal of DMPO/OOH decreased, confirming the SOD-like activity of CDs@PEG to eliminate O2
− (Fig. 2C).

Ferroptosis is always involved in CP-induced AKI, and it is usually featured as the insufficient supply of GPx, resulting in the accumulation of ROS and lipid peroxidation [32,33]. Different from previous work [34], the GPx-like activity of CDs@PEG was evaluated by the DTNB method with organic ROOH as the catalytic substrate, which excludes the interference of CAT. If the dosage of GPx is set within a suitable range to become a rate-limiting factor, the activity of GPx is negatively correlated with the content of remaining GSH which can be determined by the yellow TNB derived from the reaction between GSH and DTNB. The results showed that the reduced GSH could be consumed to initiate the reduction of the toxic peroxides by the presence of CDs@PEG within 30 min (Fig. 2D). However, the simple incubation of GSH + ROOH contribution to much slighter decline of the absorbance, may indirectly proving the GPx like activity. Moreover, a dose-dependent GPx-like enzyme activity of CDs@PEG was observed within 0 and 100 μg/mL. Our data indicated that the GPx-like activity of CDs@PEG is much higher than that of the chlorogenic acid-derived CDs [35]. Moreover, the GPx-like activity of CDs@PEG was higher than that of the natural polyphenol GA, further indicating the superior ROS scavenging potential to restrain ferroptosis (Fig. 2E). Moreover, after the addition of ROOH into the solution containing GSH and CDs@PEG (Fig. 2F), the conversion of GSH to GSSG was confirmed by liquid chromatography and the mixing of GSH with CDs@PEG do not contribute to the formation of GSSG, which proved the exact process of GPx-like activities of CDs@PEG.

Due to the similarity between -SeH and phenolic groups, the catalytic mechanism of CDs may be similar to the natural GPx following the typical ping-pong mechanism [36]. The catalytic center of CDs’ GPx activity may focused on the phenolic groups. CDs are reacted with the ROOH to generate the peroxide intermediate, which can further react with GSH to form GSSG. Further, the CDs-peroxide intermediate would recover to the initial state by reacting with GSH. Since most metal-free GPx-like nanozyme is mainly focused on selenium-containing nanomaterials [36], the enzymatic mechanism of superior GPx-like activities of CDs without selenium or sulfur was analyzed by density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The selenium-containing catalytically active site in the natural GPx enzyme is selenocysteine (Se-Cys) [37]. Herein, Se-Cys is selected as the model molecule to verify the catalytic mechanism of natural GPx. Since the first step is related to the dissociation of Se-H or phenol-like group, the bond dissociation energy (BDE) of the related group was studied by the DFT. The BDE of phenol O-H from CDs is much lower than that of Se-H (Fig. 2G), suggesting the higher H-atom donation capacity contributes to the better reduction capacity. Further, the energy barrier related to the formation of peroxide intermediate was selected to verify the potential of CDs possessing GPx-like activities. The reaction path is correlated to the association of the ROOH with the catalytic materials, and then the OH* from ROOH is exchanged with H* from the Se-Cys or CDs, which contributes to the generation of peroxide intermediate (Fig. 2G). As for CDs, the much close energy barrier (+65.34 Kcal/mol for CDs, +61.34 Kcal/mol for Se-Cys as the catalysts) and the negative free energy (–1.59 Kcal/mol) (Fig. 2H) suggests that the generation of peroxide intermediate is kinetically feasible and thermodynamically favorable. These results further confirm the possibility of the selenium-free carbon nanomaterials possessing GPx-like activities, similar to that of natural GPx. Generally, the cellular antioxidant system is based on the enzymatic reaction based on the SOD and CAT. The O2
− is disproportionated by SOD into toxic H2O2, and subsequently, the conversion of H2O2 into H2O and oxygen is realized by CAT [38]. The CAT-like activity of CDs@PEG was also investigated. Direct O2 generation is applied to study the CAT activity (Fig. S8F), similar to that of pure GA. Slight CAT-like activity of CDs@PEG was observed in a concentration-dependent manner within 0 and 200 μg/mL (Fig. 2I). Meanwhile, the produced H2O2 can be catalyzed by the metal ions to generate hydroxyl radical (Fenton reaction) in vivo, which is the most toxic ROS, owing to its high reduction potential (2.31 V) [39]. The scavenging activity of CDs@PEG on hydroxyl radical was investigated s characterized by a λmax of 536 nm for the Fe3+-Fe2+ oxidation state. Interestingly, hydroxyl radicals could also be scavenged by CDs@PEG in a concentration-dependent manner, and about 60 % of hydroxyl radicals were scavenged by CDs@PEG (Fig. 2J). Moreover, with the increase of CDs@PEG concentration, the EPR signal of DMPO/OH decreased significantly (Fig. 2K).

As a typical metal-free nanomaterial, CDs@PEG exhibited natural antioxidant system mimicking activity, including excellent SOD-like and superior GPx/CAT-like activity. Although natural antioxidant system mimicking capacities have also been reported in several ultra-small nanomaterials, such as CaPB nanoparticles [40], Cu5.4O ultra-small nanoparticles [41], and MnO2 Fenozymes [42], the transition metals contained in these nanoparticles could trigger the generation of various ROS via peroxidase and oxidase-like activity, which is inevitable due to their excellent catalytic activity. Notably, metal-free CDs@PEG displayed no peroxidase-like activity, due to the pure graphite structure without nitrogen doping (Fig. S9A). Moreover, several proper negative controls have been conducted to verify the ROS scavenging abilities, which can distinguish the natural degradation of ROS (Fig. S9B,C). The absence of peroxidase activity can be confirmed by incubating CDs incubated with H2O2 for different time with DMPO as the trapping agent (Fig. S9D, E). In this regard, CDs@PEG could be superior to the above conventional nanozymes containing transition metals. Furthermore, the GPx-like activity of selenium-free carbon nanomaterials had rarely been reported, indicating the enormous potential of CDs@PEG for the therapy of ROS-related diseases (e.g., CP-induced AKI) via the supply of GPx.

ROS are the main byproducts of oxygen metabolism, while excess ROS induce protein denaturation, DNA damage, or lipid peroxidation, resulting in various diseases [43]. ROS can be eliminated by diverse natural enzymes in vitro and in vivo. The multienzyme-like activities of CDs@PEG could protect HK-2 cells against CP-triggered oxidative damage. CDs and CDs@PEG exhibited no obvious toxicity in HK-2 cells, and the cellular viability remains over 80 % within the concentration from 12.5 to 200 μg/mL (Fig. S10A). However, about 60 % cells can be eliminated by the GA, implying the toxicity of the GA can be relieved by combining with CDs (Fig. S10B). Then the cellular protective effects of CDs and CDs@PEG were further investigated in HK-2 cell models of oxidative stress induced by lipopolysaccharide (LPS), H2O2, and CP. After the cells is treated with H2O2, the viabilities of HK-2 cells increased by about 10 % upon pre-treatment with CDs and CDs@PEG (Fig. 3 A). In addition, CDs and CDs@PEG significantly enhanced cell viability in the LPS-stimulated cell model. The increase of cellular viability reached as high as 40 % after pre-treatment with 400 μg/mL LPS in the presence of CDs or CDs@PEG (Fig. 3B). This result indirectly indicates the superior ROS scavenging ability of CDs@PEG. Compared with the cytotoxicity in HK-2 cells induced by CP, CDs or CDs@PEG (200 μg/mL) elevated the cellular viability of CP-incubated cells by about 20 %, indicating their potential for suppressing ROS-triggered ferroptosis (Fig. 3C). The protective effect of CDs@PEG on CP-induced cytotoxicity in HK-2 cells was further validated by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). The incubation of HK-2 cells with CP increased the red signal, while the red signal decreased after the pre-treatment with a ferroptosis inhibitor, ferrostatin-1 (Fer-1). In addition, the pre-treatment with CDs@PEG also increased the ratio of green/red signal (Fig. 3D), indicating that CDs@PEG could effectively alleviate CP-induced cytotoxicity. Furthermore, the perturbation of ROS in the HK-2 cells induced by CP is studied using DCFH-DA staining. The green intensity related to ROS decreased after CDs@PEG incubation and is comparable to that in cells with Fer-1 treatment (Fig. 3E). These results indicate the cytoprotective ROS scavenging effects of CDs@PEG in HK-2 cells.

The mechanism by which CDs@PEG ameliorates cellular oxidative injury was investigated. Besides ferroptosis, apoptosis is another programmed cell death [44]. The flow cytometry assay suggested that CDs@PEG mitigated CP-induced apoptosis that was similar to Fer-1 (Fig. 3G and S11). Due to the essential role of mitochondria in aerobic respiration, its dysfunction triggered by high ROS levels usually contributes to various diseases, including kidney injury [45]. Herein, the depolarization of mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) was analyzed by the fluorescent probe JC-1. The JC-1 can distinguish the transformation from highly viability mitochondria to depolarized mitochondria by alternating red to green fluorescence [46]. Almost all the JC-1 turned to monomers with green fluorescence after CP incubation. In contrast, the red FL intensity was effectively enhanced after CDs@PEG co-incubation, which is quite similar to that of the Fer-1 group, demonstrating the capacity of CDs@PEG to reverse the CP-induced depolarized MMP (Fig. 3F).

Ferroptosis is a typically necrotic cell death featured as iron-dependent, lipid peroxidation-driven membrane destruction [7]. GPx4 plays an essential role in detoxifying ROOH in mammalian cells via catalyzing the reduction of ROOH collaborated with GSH or other small molecular thiols [47]. The content of intracellular ROOH was analyzed by the Liperfluo probe. As shown in Fig. 4 A, compared with normal HK-2 cells, the green FL increased obviously after pre-treatment with CP, indicating a dramatic increase in the content of ROOH. The co-incubation with CDs@PEG nanozyme or Fer-1 decreased the concentration of ROOH, and the elimination efficacy was robust (Fig. 4B, C) due to the inherent GPX-like activity of CDs@PEG. Since intracellular GSH is an essential co-factor of GPx4 that de-toxify ROOH [47], the content of GSH was also investigated by monobromobimane (MBB) staining. The intracellular GSH declined obviously after CP treatment, while it was reversed by Fer-1 incubation. The relative activity of GSH treated with CDs@PEG was similar to that of the Fer-1 treated group (Fig. 4D, E). Moreover, the reduction of NADPH levels induced by CP was markedly relieved by the Fer-1 or CDs@PEG (Fig. 4F), suggesting that CDs@PEG possesses ameliorative effects on CP-induced toxicity by inhibiting ferroptosis [48]. Furthermore, the iron level is highly related to lipid peroxidation during ferroptosis, which can be evaluated by labile iron pool (LIP) assay [49]. CP treatment increased free Fe2+ levels, as indicated by the FL intensity of the FerroOrange stained cells (Fig. 4G, H). As shown in the results of flow cytometry, the excessive accumulation of free Fe2+ levels induced by CP was significantly reversed by Fer-1 or CDs@PEG (Fig. 4I and S12).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo toxicity assessment of CDs@PEG
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the biocompatibility of CDs@PEG, healthy ICR mice were intravenously injected with PBS or CDs@PEG. It was found that no tissue damage or organic lesion emerged in CDs@PEG-injected cohorts (Fig. 5 A). Further, the liver/kidney function (Fig. S13A, B) and hematologic indices (Fig. S13C) were within the typical values and consistent with control indices. These results indicated that CDs@PEG showed favorable biocompatibility and no obvious toxicity in vivo, revealing the safety of CDs@PEG for further therapeutic utilization.

The targetable renal accumulation of CDs@PEG is critical for AKI treatment. Rhodamine B (RB) is a typical FL probe for visualizing drug distribution and nanomaterial tracing in vivo. Thus, RB-labeled CDs@PEG were intravenously injected in healthy and AKI mice. IVIS Lumina imaging system was used to investigate the targetability and biodistribution of as-prepared CDs@PEG in tissue samples. The imaging results suggested that CDs@PEG were mainly distributed in the liver and kidneys of healthy mice (Fig. 5B, C). The FL signals peaked at approximately 12 h post-injection. Remarkably, the FL intensity slightly decreased but remained high at 24 h post-injection. Consistently, CDs@PEG mainly enriched around the livers and kidneys of CP-AKI mice, while a smaller portion was allocated to other organs than that in healthy cohorts. When the liver is injured by cisplatin, the hepatic vascular permeability is increased [50], which may lead to the higher enrichment of RB-labeled CDs@PEG in the liver of cisplatin-injected mice compared to that in the normal mice. Moreover, we found that FL intensities from kidney tissues of CDs@PEG-injected CP-AKI mice were more intensive than normal mice and did not display significant attenuation even 24 h post-injection (Fig. 5D, E). Indeed, nanoparticles administered intravenously can be rapidly focused on damaged tissue and exhibit higher uptake due to increased microvascular permeability in AKI mice [51]. Furthermore, damaged renal function in CP-AKI mice reduced the clearance of CDs@PEG, leading to passive storage of CDs@PEG in the renal tubules for extended treatment time [52]. Therefore, these data demonstrate that CDs@PEG specifically accumulated and had good retention capacity in injured kidneys.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo therapeutic efficiency of CDs@PEG in CP-AKI mice.
ce:section.ce:para: Ferroptosis is a ROS-dependent oxidative stress-induced cell death form closely associated with iron overload and lipid peroxidation [53]. Multiple studies have shown the induction of ferroptosis in multi-stimulus-triggered AKI. Suppression of ferroptosis by regulating iron homeostasis is a novel potential strategy for treating AKI [54–56]. In this study, we first evaluated the therapeutic efficacy of CDs@PEG in AKI mice (Fig. 6 A). Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining showed sediment of denatured proteins in tubule forms (marked as triangles), namely AKI cast, in AKI mice. Notably, CDs@PEG and Fer-1 treatment mitigated AKI in mice, as indicated by rare casts in histopathologic examination (Fig. 6B). Consistently, AKI mice displayed elevated serum levels of renal function indicators, including blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and serum creatinine (Cre). In contrast, levels of serum BUN and Cre in CDs@PEG-treated AKI mice were notably lower than in AKI mice (Fig. 6C, D). Subsequently, we further explored the antioxidant ability of CDs@PEG in vivo. CDs@PEG repressed renal ROS production in AKI mice, indicating CDs@PEG could possess the antioxidant potential against renal oxidative damage (Fig. 6E). Accordingly, the TUNEL assay suggested that CDs@PEG and Fer-1 repressed cell apoptosis in the kidneys of AKI mice (Fig. 6F). Similar anti-AKI efficacy was observed in Fer-1-treated cohorts. Overall, we initially suppose that CDs@PEG could ameliorate AKI by restraining oxidative stress and inhibiting ferroptosis in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs@PEG inhibits ferroptosis in the kidneys of CP-AKI mice.
ce:section.ce:para: Ferroptosis is accompanied by mitochondrial morphology changes [57]. TEM results showed that CDs@PEG or Fer-1 could protect kidneys against CP-induced mitochondrial cristae disappearance and outer membrane rupture (Fig. 7 A). 4-hydroxynonenal (4-HNE) and Malondialdehyde (MDA) are lipid peroxidation products triggering ferroptosis in vivo [53] . Immunohistochemical (IHC) staining showed that CDs@PEG or Fer-1 suppressed the renal expression of 4-HNE in AKI mice (Fig. 7B). GPx4 is a unique selenocysteine-containing antioxidant enzyme functioning to reduce lipid peroxides to phospholipid alcohols that induce ferroptosis [53]. Subsequently, we evaluated the ameliorative effect of CDs@PEG on renal lipid peroxidation by determining renal GPx4 activities and GSH levels in mice. The activity of GPx in kidneys was restored in CDs@PEG-treated cohorts (Fig. 7C). Also, CDs@PEG elevated the renal GSH levels in AKI mice (Fig. 7D). Meanwhile, renal MDA levels were reduced in AKI mice upon CDs@PEG or Fer-1 treatment (Fig. 7E). Acyl-CoA synthetase long-chain family member 4 (ACSL4) and prostaglandin-endoperoxide synthase 2 (PTGS2) are indicators of ferroptosis [58]. Notably, CDs@PEG and Fer-1 significantly repressed the renal protein expressions of ACSL4 and PTGS2 in vivo (Fig. 7F). Together, these results suggest that CDs@PEG could repress oxidative stress-induced ferroptosis and delay disease progression in CP-AKI.

Furthermore, ferroptosis is a form of cell death owing to uncontrolled membrane lipid peroxidation [7]. Nevertheless, GPx4 acts as a phospholipid hydroperoxidase, taking GSH as a substrate to reduce phospholipid hydroperoxide production to the corresponding phospholipid alcohol [59]. Meanwhile, xCT (also commonly known as SLC7A11) could transit extracellular cystine into cellular for synthesizing GSH [60]. Mechanistically, western blotting analysis indicated that the impaired GPx4/xCT antioxidant axis in the kidneys of AKI mice was significantly restored by CDs@PEG injection (Fig. 7G). The ROS mainly include O2 ·−, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and hydroxyl radicals (·OH). ·OH is the major ROS leading to lipid peroxidation and formed through the Fenton reaction between iron ions (Fe2+) and H2O2 [61]. Decreased ROS levels reduce lipid peroxidation production, thus alleviating ferroptosis in injured kidneys [49]. Transferrin receptor 1 (TfR1) and ferritin represent well-known cellular iron regulators. TfR1 is a transmembrane glycoprotein responsible for internalizing transferrin-bound iron after releasing it into the cytoplasm and storing it in a non-toxic form in a metalloprotein complex called ferritin. Also, ROS-mediated autophagy increases intracellular iron levels through FTH1 and TfR1 regulation [62]. Indeed, the present study showed that CDs@PEG effectively eliminated ROS in vivo and in vitro. Notably, the immunoblotting results suggested that downregulated FTH1 expression and elevated TfR1 expression in the kidneys of AKI mice were reversed by CDs@PEG injection (Fig. 7H), which is consistent with CDs@PEG-induced reduction of intracellular LIP in CP-incubated HK-2 cells in vitro. Altogether, these data suggest that CDs@PEG represses ferroptosis by activating the GPx4/xCT antioxidant axis and inhibiting ROS-induced oxidative stress.

In summary, the metal-free and phenolic functionalized CDs that can mimic SOD, GPX, and CAT-like activities were first fabricated by a hydrothermal environment of GA and PEI, which can mimic the intracellular antioxidant defense system. The SOD activity of CDs is determined to be extremely high (18187 U/mg). Due to superior intracellular antioxidant system mimicking activities, the selective enrichment in the kidney, and ferroptosis inhibition contribute to the remarkable in vivo therapeutic effect. Herein, the as-prepared phenolic functionalized CDs hold great potential for nano-catalytic therapy of CP-mediated AKI, which might provide valuable guidance for designing highly efficient metal-free carbon nanomaterials for ameliorating ROS-related diseases.

The natural water-soluble GA (120 mg) and PEI1800 (160 mg) were added into deionized water (30 mL). The pH was adjusted to 12 by NaOH. After 10 min of ultrasonic dissolving, the clear precursor solution is transferred to an autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 6 h. The reactors were naturally cooled to room temperature following the reaction. Subsequently, the solution was filtered through 0.22 μm membrane filters and dialyzed against deionized water (MW: 3500 Da) for 72 h. Finally, the obtained CDs were freeze-dried and stored for future use.

Subsequently, 50 mg CDs and 100 mg mPEG-NHS (MW: 2000 Da) were mixed in 40 mL of water and stirred with a magnetic force for 24 h. The solution was then dialyzed against deionized water for 12 h using a dialysis bag (MW: 3500 Da). Finally, CDs@PEG were freeze-dried and stored for future use.

Human proximal tubule epithelial HK-2 cells were inoculated at 5 × 103 cells/well in a 96-well cell plate for 24 h. Afterward, CDs@PEG (0–200 μg/mL) were added to the wells individually. After further incubation for 24 h, the MTT solution (0.5 mg/mL) was added and incubated for 4 h. Then the medium was removed, and the formed formazan was dissolved by 150 μL of DMSO. The absorption of the solution at 490 nm was measured with a microplate spectrophotometer.

As for the cytoprotective experiments, the cells were seeded into a 96-well plate, pre-treated with CDs@PEG for 30 min, and then treated with CP for Fer-1 (20 μmol/L). After 24 h, the cellular viability was determined using the MTT assay.

After the cells were treated with CDs@PEG, CP, or Fer-1 at the same experimental conditions, the cells were washed with PBS two times and stained with DCFH-DA to analyze the intracellular ROS levels. Meanwhile, the treated cells are stained with 15 μL calcein-AM and 15 μL propidium iodide (PI) in 1 × assay buffer for 20 min to perform the Live/dead assay. The cells were stained with JC-1 (10 μM) for 30 min at 37 °C to monitor the MMP. The lipid peroxidation was determined by staining the treated cells with Liperfluo. In addition, the FerroOrange staining was utilized to access the intracellular iron pool. The treated cells were stained with MBB (20 μM) for 15 min at 37 °C. After the treated cells were stained with the above different dyes, the FL images of treated cells were taken by the Leica DMi8 CLSM (Wetzlar, Germany), and relative intensity was determined by the ImageJ software.

After the cells were treated with CDs@PEG, CP, or Fer-1 at identical experimental details, the cells were incubated with trypsin, and the resuspended cells were rinsed with PBS and stained with AnnexinV-FITC and PI according to the instructions of the AnnexinV-FITC Apoptosis Detection Kit. The cell behavior was subsequently analyzed by Novo Cyte D2060R flow cytometry (Agilent, Santa Clara, CA, USA).

The LIP assay was used to assess intracellular free iron levels according to the methods described by Prus and Fibach [47]. Briefly, the cells were treated with 5 μM calcein-acetoxymethyl ester for 15 min, then incubated with or without 100 μM deferiprone for 1 h. The intracellular iron levels were analyzed using a flow cytometer. The difference in the mean FL intensity of cells between deferiprone-treated and not treated reflects the amount of intracellular free iron.

The NADPH levels in HK-2 cells were assessed using the NADP+/NADPH assay kit. Briefly, the treated cells were lysed in 200 μL NADP+/NADPH extraction buffer and centrifuged at 12,000 × g for 10 min. The supernatants were collected and heat-treated for 30 min at 60 °C for NADP+ decomposition. 50 μL of samples or standards were incubated with 100 μL G6PDH reaction mixture in a 96-well plate for 10 min at 37 °C. Then, 10 μL of NAPDH developer was added into each well; the reaction was allowed to proceed for 20 min, and the absorbance at 450 nm was measured using a microplate reader.

In order to label CDs@PEG by RB, the NPs were incubated with RB-PEG2000-NHS at room temperature overnight. The solution was then dialyzed against deionized water for 12 h using a dialysis bag (MW: 3500 Da). Finally, the RB-CDs@PEG were freeze-dried and stored for future use. In this study, healthy and AKI mice were injected intravenously with RB-labeled CDs@PEG (1 mg/mL, 200 μL). Afterward, mice were euthanized, and vital organs were obtained at selected time sites after drug administration. The FL imaging was performed using an IVIS Lumina imaging system (Xenogen Corporation, Alameda, CA, USA). Living Image 4.2 software was used to quantify the fluorescence signal of each organ. Rhodamine B: λem = 525 nm, λex = 488 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Western blotting, immunohistochemistry (IHC), TUNEL staining, and dihydroethidium (DHE)
ce:section.ce:para: Protein from liver tissue or cell samples was extracted with M−PER Mammalian Protein Extraction Reagent containing protease/phosphatase inhibitors. The Protein Assay Kit (BCA) determines the protein concentration. Protein samples (20–40 μg) spiked with loading buffer were denatured thermally in sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) loading buffer and separated on SDS-PAGE gels. Then the separated proteins were transferred to nitrocellulose membranes and closed in 5 % skim milk for 2 h. The primary antibody was incubated overnight. Immunoblot strips were washed with TBS containing 0.1 % Tween 20 three times (10 min each), then incubated with horseradish peroxidase-conjugated secondary antibody for 1 h and washed three times (10 min each). The blots were visualized using the chemiluminescent method. Densitometric analysis was performed using ImageJ software (version 1.51). Immunohistochemistry of the tumor tissues was performed as described previously. The 10 % formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded kidney tissue sections were dehydrated in gradient ethanol treatments. The processed sections were subjected to antigen repair in citrate buffer (pH 6.0). Slides were blocked with 2.5 % normal goat serum and incubated with primary antibody at 4 °C. The slides were washed three times with PBS. According to the manufacturer's instructions, the slides were stained with SP Rabbit & Mouse HRP Kit for staining and photographed with a microscope (Olympus, Japan).

TUNEL assay was carried out as previously described with minor modifications. The TUNEL staining technique was performed using an In Situ Cell Death Detection Kit according to the manufacturer’s introduction. Frozen kidney tissue sections (approximately 5 μm thickness) were washed with PBS and stained with 1 mM DHE for 30 min to detect superoxide formation. After washing with PBS three times every 5 min, tissues were incubated with DAPI, and fluorescence was observed under a fluorescence microscope (Olympus, Japan).
The description of other materials and methods used in this study is provided in the Supporting Information.
